m•ca PREFACE This sixth edition of the National Commercial & Industrial Insulation Standards Manual is dedicated in the memory of William O. Mauldin, Jr. His commitment to this manual and the insulation industry is an inspiration for all current and future members of our industry. The sixth edition of the manual has been revised and expanded. The insulation materials and properties section has been updated to include the changes and improvements that have been made to the insulation materials by the manufacturers. The materials tables have been expanded to include ASTM numbers. Several of the plates have been re-designed to reflect the new materials available to the industry and to reflect changes in insulation application procedures that have occurred since the previous edition. New plates have been added where appropriate to reflect new procedures. The insulation specification section has been the MICA plat The firestopp of this the new numbering system and to show how •corporated in the specification writing process. , as been expanded to provide a greater overview r ergt specification requirements. The Editorial Board has made every effort to eliminate errors and incon- sistencies and to provide proper credit to all sources used in the manual. I personally want to thank the members of the Editorial Board for their diligence in revising this manual. Their personal time, commitment, and dedication to seeing this process through to its completion is most commendable. The hours given and the personal sacrifices made are deeply appreciated. This revision was accomplished through the support from many segments of our industry. As Chairman, I wish to acknowledge everyone who provided this support and to thank them for their efforts in helping us provide our industry with a manual that can be used for the betterment of our profession. James D. Pfister, Chairman Standards Editing Committee, Sixth Edition Copyright Midwest Insulation Contractors Association 1979 Revised 1983 Revised 1988 Rev,sed 1993 Revised 1999 Revised 2006

muca SECTION I INTRODUCTION 1.1 SCOPE NATIONAL COMMERCIAL AND INDUSTRIAL INSULATION STANDARDS contains updated information relative to the design, specification and installation of thermal insulation* on piping, fittings, ductwork, equipment and vessels, found within commercial heating/ ventilating/cooling and plumbing systems**, and industrial power and process systems. ** Information on firestopping systems is also included. *** 1.2 INTENT This publication represents MICA'S continued program of Standards revisions and specialized editions serving the insulation industry and its customers as: 1. An updated source of technical information concerning commercial and insulation products and practices. 2. A means by which installation practices that are commonly accepted throughout the industry can be understood and followed. 1.3 RESOURCES These publications are closely edited by active members of the Midwest Insulation Contractors Association. The information presented is developed from the collective experience and expertise of insulation contractors and others. Manufacturers' data and suggested application instructions are presented only as proven against the framework of the contractors' experience. References throughout the text to other resources (i.e. insulation thickness computer programs, ASHRAE journals, ASTM, etc.) are highly recommended for a more thorough knowledge of the many facets of the insulation industry. * See Section II ** See Section Ill * See Section X

1.4 STANDARDS ORGANIZATION AND USE The Standards may be used as a basic reference for insulation design and specification by engineers, architects, contractors and consultants in the field of commercial and industrial insulation. Sections II (materials), III (design factors), IV (application illustrations), VI (specification writing), X (firestopping systems), the Glossary (industry terminology) and the Appendix, are designed to provide general background information and updated materials data for the specifying engineer. Sections VI (specification writing), VIII (maintenance), IX finsulation thickness programs) and the Property Tables are recommended to veteran contractors and engineers as a point of reference for continued growth and research. Sections IV (application illustrations), V (specialized applications), VII (project coordination) and the Glossary have been prepared to define standard industry terminology, application methods and policies which would be mutually understood by specification writers, contractors and installation personnel, and referenced specifically to this publication in written specifications. Specifying engineers will find it expedient to make reference to the MICA sample specification found in Section VI. 1.5 PROPERTY TABLES AND APPLICATION PLATES The property tables in Section II are presented as a guide for the selection of the appropriate generic materials for given applications using only those properties required. The final choice of a particular manufactured product, however, must be made by comparing competitive products and verifying their properties. The information used in the tables was obtained from several sources, primarily manufacturers' published literature. The illustrations in Section IV and V represent applications of insulation deemed acceptable by the insulation industry. However, the purpose is not to specify any particular brand of material or combination of materials and accessories, but to illustrate how these various choices would appear when correctly applied.

Accessories and finishes depicted are often interchangeable with several other types, or the illustration may depict a more finished application than is necessary (i.e. concealed insulation often does not require the types of finishes shown on the plate). These choices are at the specifier's discretion. Plates may be used by the specifying engineer by making reference to the plate. page and edition number in the specification and by providing copies of the plates with necessary modification notes, as part of the specification document. See Section VI. 1.6 GLOSSARY AND APPENDIX The definitions and interpretations listed in the Glossary (Section X') are industry defined and should be reviewed before used in specifications. The Appendix presents commonly used engineering tools, conversions and measurement methods. Consult engineering manuals for more complete tables. 1.7 METRICATION The 1999, fifth edition of the manual was modified to include metric measures in the text, tables, formulas, and application plates. The inclusion of metric measures demonstrated MICA's efforts to support the federal government and the construction industry in their commitment to adopt the metric system as the fundamental standards for measurement. With the sixth edition we continue to inform the reader of the metric conversion formulas used throughout the manual where appropriate. We have also attempted to indicate the conversion formulas used and sources cited in the development of the appendix tables. The tables are presented in metric and imperial measures. Accuracy in conversion is solely based on the formulas used in conversion. Different conversion formulas and different rounding of numbers will result in different results from those shown in the appendix. Sole reliance on the conversion tables for metric accuracy is discouraged.

1.8 CD-ROM VERSION OF THE MANUAL Accompanying the sixth edition of the manual is a CD that contains the complete manual in electronic form. This CD makes the manual a "user friendly", interactive, live document for the user. The application illustration plates (Sections IV & V) are editable and were developed to allow the specifying engineer to reference specific insulation materials and accessories to be used on each system. The contractor, in turn, can use the plates for detailed submittal purposes. Both the speciMng engineer and contractor can use the plates to insert insulation manufacturers' materials, accessories, and finishes for specific insulation applications. These documents can be saved and modified for future use. 1

muca SECTION II INSULATION MATERIALS AND PROPERTIES 2.1 DEFINITION OF INSULATION Insulation is defined as those materials or combinations of materials which retard the flow of heat energy by performing one or more of the following functions: I. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7. 8. 9. Conserve energy by reducing heat loss or gain. Control surface temperatures for personnel protection and comfort. Facilitate temperature control of a process. Prevent vapor flow and water condensation on cold surfaces. Increase operating efficiency of heating/ventilating/cooling, plumbing, steam, process and power systems found in commercial and industrial installations. Prevent or reduce damage to equipment from exposure to fire or corrosive atmospheres. Assist mechanical systems in meeting USDA (FDA) criteria in food and cosmetic plants. Control noise for personnel protection and comfort. Aides freeze protection for piping and equipment. IO. Protect the environment by reducing emissions of particulates and greenhouse gasses. The temperature range wmin which the term "thermal insulation" will apply is from -73.30C (-1 OOOF) to 815.SOC (15000F). All applica- tions below -73.30C (-1000F) are termed "cryogenic," and those above 815.60C (15000F) are termed "refractory." Thermal insulation is further divided into three general application ranges as follows: A. Low Temperature Thermal Insulation 1. 15.60C through OOC (600F through 320F) — i.e. Cold or chilled water. 11

2. 3. 4. B. C. -0.60C through -39.40C (310F through -390F) — i.e. Refrigeration or glycol. -40.OOC through -73.30C (400F through -IOOOF) — i.e. Refrigeration or brine. -73.90C through -267.80c (-101 OF through 4500F). i.e. Cryogenic. Intermediate Temperature Thermal Insulation 1. 16.10C through 99.40C (610F through 21 IOF) — i.e. Hot water and steam condensate. 2. 100.OOC through 315.eoc (2120F through 6000F) — i.e. Steam, high temperature hot water. High Temperature Thermal Insulation 1. 316.10C through 815.60C (6010Ffrough 15000B — i.e. Turbines, breechings, sæcks, exhatßs, incinerators, boilers. 2.2 2.2.1 GENERIC TYPES AND FORMS OF INSULATION Insulation will be discussed in this manual according to its genemc types and forms. The type indicates composition (i.e. glass, plas- tic) and internal structure (i.e. cellular, fibrous). The form implies overall shape or application (i.e. board, blanket, pipe insulation). TYPES I. 2. 3. Fibrous Insulation — composed of small diameter fibers which finely divide the air space. The fibers may be perpen- dicular or horizontal to the surface being insulated, and they may or may not be bonded together. Silica, rock wool, slag wool and alumina silica fibers are used. The most widely used insulations of this type are glass fiber and mineral wool. Closed Cell Insulation — composed of small individual cells separated from each other. The cellular material may be glass, elastomeric, or foamed plastic such as polystyrene (extruded), polyurethane, polyisocyanurate. or polyolefin resins. Granular Insulation — composed of small nodules which contain voids or hollow spaces. It is not considered a true closed cell material since gas can be transferred between the individual spaces. This type may be produced as a loose or pourable material. or combined with a binder and fibers to make a rigid insulation, Examples of these insulations are calcium silicate, expanded vermiculite. perlite. cellulose, diatomaceous earth and expanded polystyrene.

2.2.2 FORMS Insulation is produced in a variety of forms suitable for specific functions and applications. The combined form and type of insulation determine its proper method of installation. The forms most widely used are: 1. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. Rigid boards, blocks, sheets, and pre-formed shapes such as pipe insulation, curved segment, lagging, etc. Cellular, granular. and fibrous insulations are produced in these forms. Flexible sheets and pre-formed shapes. Cellular and fibrous insulations are produced in these forms. Flexible blankets. Fibrous insulations are produced in flexible blankets. Cements (insulating and finishing). Produced from fibrous and granular insulations and cement, they may be of the hydraulic setting or air drying type. Foam: Poured or froth foam used to fill irregular areas and voids. Spray used for flat surfaces. Removable and reusable blankets or pads. 2.3 PROPERTIES OF INSULATION Not all properties are significant for all materials or applications. Therefore, many are not included in manufacturers' published literature or in the Table of Properties which follows in this section. In some applications, however, omitted properties may assume extreme importance (i.e. when insulations must be compatible with chemically corrosive atmospheres.) If the property is significant for an application and the measure of that property cannot be found in manufacturers' literature. effort should be made to obtain the information directly from the manu- facturer, testing laboratory, or insulation contractors association. The following properties are referenced only according to their significance in meeting design criteria of specific applications. More detailed definitions of the properties themselves can be found in the Glossary (Section Xl).

1. THERMAL PROPERTIES OF INSULATION Thermal properties are the primary consideration in choosing insulations. Refer to the Glossary for definitions. a. b. c. d. e. Temperature limits: Upper & lower temperatures within which the material must retain all its properties. Thermal conductance "C": The rate of heat flow for the actual thickness of a material. Thermal conductivity "K": The property of an insulation material which measures its ability to conduct heat. Emissivity "E": The ratio of radiation emitted by a surface. Significant when the surface temperature of the insulation must be regulated as with moisture condensation or per- sonnel protection. Thermal resistance "R-Value": The ability of a thickness of insulation to retard heat flow. f. Thermal transmittance "U": The overall transmission of heat flow through a "system". 2. MECHANICAL AND CHEMICAL PROPERTIES OF INSULATION Properties other than therrnal must be considered when choosing materials for specific applications. Among them are: a. Appearance: Important in exposed areas and for coding b. c. d. e. f. g. purposes. Breaking load: In some installations the insulation material must "bridge" over a discontinuity in its support. Capillarity: Must be considered when material may be in contact with liquids. Chemical reaction: Potential fire hazards exist in areas where volatile chemicals are present. Corrosion resis- tance must also be considered. Chemical resistance: Significant when the atmosphere is salt or chemical laden. Coefficient of expansion and contraction: Enters into the design and spacing of expansion/contraction joints and/or the use of multiple layer insulation applications. Combustibility: One of the measures of a material's contribution to a fire hazard. 1

h. j. k. Compressive strength: Important if the insulation must support a load or withstand mechanical abuse without crushing. If, however, cushioning or filling in space is needed as in expansion/conü•action joints, low compressive strength materials are specified. Density: A material's density affects other properties of that material. especially thermal properties. Dimensional stability: Significant when the material is exposed to atmospheric and mechanical abuse such as twisting or vibration from thermally expanding or contract- ing pipe and equipment. Fire retardancy: Flame spread and smoke developed ratings should be considered. Hygroscopicity: Tendency of a material to absorb water vapor from the air. m. pH (alkalinity or acidity): Significant when corrosive atmospheres are present. Also insulation must not con- tribute to corrosion of the system. See Section III. n. Resistance to ultraviolet light: Significant if application is outdoors. o. Resistance to fungal or bacterial growth: Is necessary in all applications where condensation could be an issue. p. Shrinkage: Significant on applications involving cements and mastics. q. Sound absorption coefficient: Must be considered when sound attenuation is required, as it is in radio stations, some hospital areas, etc. r. Sound transmission loss value: Significant when constructing a soundbarrier. s. Toxicity: Must be considered in food processing plants and potential fire hazard areas. 2.4 MAJOR INSULATION MATERIALS The following is a general inventory of the characteristics and properties of major insulation materials used in commercial and industrial installations. See the Insulation Property Tables at the end of Section II for a comparative review. It is recommended to review the most current version of the manu- facturer's technical literature.

1. CALCIUM SILICATE Calcium silicate is a granular insulation made of lime and silica, reinforced with organic and inorganic fibers and molded into rigid forms. Service temperature range covered is 37.80C to 648.90C (IOOOF to 12000F). Flexural strength is good. Calcium silicate is water absorb- ent. However, it can be dried out without deterioration. The material is noncombustible and used primarily on hot piping and surfaces. Jacketing is field applied. 2. GLASS a. Fibrous: Available as flexible blanket, rigid board, pipe b. insulation, wicking pipe insulation, and other pre-molded shapes. Service temperature range is 40.00C to 537.80C (400F to IOOOOF). Fibrous glass is of nearly neutral PH. The product is noncombustible or of limited combustibility, and has good sound absorption qualities. Cellular: Available in board and block form capable of being fabricated into pipe insulation and various shapes. Service temperature range is -267.80C to 482.20C (-4500F to 9000F). Good structural strength, poor impact resistance. Material is noncombustible, non-absorptive and resistant to many chemicals. a. MINERAL FIBER (ROCK AND SLAG WOOL) Rock and/or slag fibers are bonded together with a heat resistant binder to produce mineral fiber or wool available in loose blanket, board, pipe insulation, and molded shapes. Upper temperature limit can reach 1037.8 oc (19000F). The material has a practically neutral PH, is nor-y combustible, and has good sound control qualities. 4. EXPANDED SILICA, OR PERLITE Perlite is made from an inert siliceous volcanic rock com- bined with water. The rock is expanded by heating, causing the water to vaporize and the rock volume to expand. This creates a cellular structure of minute air cells surrounded by vitrified product. Added binders resist moisture peneü•a- tion and inorganic fibers reinforce the sü•ucture. The material has low shrinkage and high resistance to substrate cormsion. Perlite is noncombustible and operates in intermediate and high temperature ranges. The product is available in rigid pre-formed shapes and blocks. 16

5. ELASTOMERIC Foamed resins combined with elastomers produce a fiexibte cellular material. Available in tubular, sheet, and roll form (with and without factory applied adhesives). Elastomeric insulations possess good fabrication characteristics and low water and vapor permeability. Service temperature range is 1820C (-297B to 160.50C (2570F). Elastomeric insulation is cost efficient for low temperature applications with no jacketing necessary. Resiliency is high. Consideration should be made for fire retardancy of the material. 6. FOAMED PLASTIC Insulations produced from foaming plastic resins create predominately closed-cell insulations. Therrnal efficiency can decline over time as tie gas ü•apped within the cellular struc- ture is eventually replaced by air. Check manufacturers' data. Foamed plastics are light weight with excellent mois- ture resistance and cutting characteristics. The chemical content varies with each manufacturer and can include (but not limited to) polyisocyanurates, polyolefins, and polystyrenes. Available in pre-formed shapes and boards, foamed plastics are generally used in the low and lower intermediate service temperature range -182.80C to 148.SOC (-2970F to 3000F). Consideration should be made for fire retardancy of the material. 7. REFRACTORY FIBER Refractory ceramic fiber (RCF) insulations are made from alumina and silica. The material is manufactured in needled flexible blanket — with no binders, or rigid board — with high temperature binders. Thermal shock resistance is high. Service temperature limits reach 12G00C (2800CF) with a melting point of 17600C (32000F). The material is noncombustible. The use and design of refractory range materials is an engineering art in its own right and is not treated fully in this manual, although some refractory products can be installed using application methods illustrated here.

2.5 8. SOLUBLE FIBER Soluble fiber insulations are made from calcia, silica, mag- nesia (CSM) chemistry, specifically formulated to enhance biosolubility. Used as an alternate to RCF insulations in high temperature applications. Commonly found in unbound bulk fiber and needled flexible blanket forms. CSM offers excellent insulating properties and fire retardance for appli- cations with continuous temperature to 9820C (18000F), and intermittent exposures to 10930C (20000F). 9. INSULATING CEMENT Insulating and finishing cements are a mixture of various insulating fibers and binders with water and cement, to form a soft plastic mass for application on irregular sur- faces. Insulation values are moderate. Cements may be applied to high temperature surfaces. finishing cements or one-coat cements are used in the lower intermediate range and as a finish to other insulation applications. Check each manufacturer for shrinkage and adhesion properties. PROTECTIVE COVERINGS AND FINISHES The efficiency and service of insulation is-directly dependent upon its protection from moisture entry and mechanical and chemical damage. Choices of jacketing and finish materials are based upon the mechanical, chemical, thermal and moisture conditions of the installation, as well as cost and appearance requirements. Protective coverings are divided into six main functional types. 1. WEATHER-BARRIERS (See Table 2A) The basic function of the weather-barrier is to prevent the entry of water, ice, snow or atmospheric residue into the insulation. Sunlight and ozone can also damage certain insulations. Applications may be either jacketing of metal or plastic, or a coating of weather-barrier mastic. Jacketing must be overlapped sufficiently to prevent the ingress of water. Avoid the use of plastic jacketing materials with low resistance to ultraviolet rays unless protective measures are taken.

2.6 5. APPEARANCE COVERINGS AND FINISHES Various coatings, finishing cements, frting covers and jackets are chosen primarily for uneir appearance value in exposed areas. 6. HYGIENIC COVERINGS Coatings and jackets must present a smooth surface which resists fungal or bacterial growth in food process areas. High temperature steam or high pressure water wash down conditions require jackets with high mechanical strength and temperature ranges. (See Section III.) PROPERTIES OF PROTECTIVE COVERINGS The properties of jacketing and mastic materials that must be considered to meet the aforementioned functions are: 1. CHEMICAL COMPATIBILITY The chemical make-up of coverings must be compatible with the insulation material over which they are applied, as well as resistant to elements in the environment such as industrial chemicals, salt, air and ultraviolet or infrared light. 2. RESISTANCE TO INTERNAL AND EXTERNAL MOVEMENT This property is significant if the covering must absorb or compensate for thermal expansion and contraction of the insulation it covers (i.e. shrinkage of high temperature insulation) or if the system vibration must be considered. 3. TEMPERATURE RANGE OF THE FINISH OR COVERING The temperature range must be compatible with the service temperature of the insulation surface. 4. VAPOR PERMEABILITY Permeability should be considered for below ambient or dual temperature systems. The covering should prevent the passage of moisture vapor into the insulation. 5. FIRE RETARDANCE Flame spread and smoke developed ratings should be considered.

2.7 ACCESSORIES The term "accessories" is applied to devices or materials serving one or more of the following functions. Improper design or application of one or more of these accessories is a signifi- cant factor in the failure of insulation systems. 1. Securements of insulation and jacketing: As most insulations are not structural materials they must be supported, secured, fastened or bonded in place. Securements must be compatible with insulation and jacketing materials. Possible choices include: a. Studs and pins. b. Staples, serrated fasteners, rivets and screws. c. Clips. d. Wire or straps. e. Self-adhering laps. f. Tape*. g. Adhesives* *Ambient temperature and humidity conditions affect the efficiency of tapes and adhesives on certain installations. Check the properties of temperature range and vapor permeability before choosing adhesives. And, wherever possible, use mechanical securements. 2. Reinforcement for cements and mastics: Mastics and cements should be reinforced to provide mechanical strength The following materials can be used: a. Fiber fabrics. b. Expanded metal lath. c. Metal meshes. d. Wire netting. Compatibility of materials must be considered to prevent corrosion. 3. Flashing: Materials which direct the flow of liquids away from the insulation may be constructed of metal, plastic or mastic. 4. Stiffening: Metal lath and wire netting can be applied on high- temperature surfaces before hea',y density insulation is applied. 5. Supports: Pipe supports and accessories may be supplied in part or totally by the insulation contractor. Insulation treatment at points of support are illustrated on Plates No. 5, 6 and 7. Accessories at points of support are: a. Heavy density insulation inserts. b. Pipe support saddles and shoes. 21

2.8 c. Insulation and metal shields used to protect insulation. d. Wood blocks or dowels. e. Pre-insulated pipe supports using a load bearing insulating core with outer facing for integration into the system. Insulation support rings on vertical piping and vessels should be supplied by the piping or vessel contractor, as field welding on coded piping or vessels voids the original coding by the manu- facturers. See Plate No. 9 for treatment. 6. Sealing and caulking: A variety of sealers, caulking and tapes are available for sealing vapor and weather-barrier jackets, joints and protrusions. These products are manufactured in a large range of temperature and vapor permeability properties. Some are designed specifically for use with one type of insula- tion or manufacturer's products. 7. Expansion/Contraction compensation: Accessories used in the design of expansion/contraction joints, etc. include: a. Manufactured overlapping or slip joints. b. Bedding compounds and flexible sealers. See Plates No. 9, 29, 2SA and 41A for insulation treatrnents. SUMMARY - INSULATION MATERIALS & APPL.mTIONS WITHIN THE GENERAL TEMPERATURE RANGES Choices of the materials available within each temperature range are based on design conditions (other than unerrnal) of the installation. See Section III for more detailed design information. LOW TEMPERATURE RANGE -267.80c to 15.60C (-4500F to GOOF) The major design problems on low and extreme low temperature installations are moisture penetration and operating efficiency. Ideally, the insulation material should absorb no moisture. Vapor-retarders are extensively used, but in practice it is difficult to achieve the perfect barrier. The pressure of the vapor flow from the warm outside surface to the cooler inside surface is such that, even with waterproof insulation, vapor may diffuse through the material, enter through unsealed joints or cracks, and condense, then freeze and cause damage. Since the cost of refrigeration is higher than the cost of heating, more insulation is often justified in low temperature applications. Extra thicknesses of insulation, even beyond what would be economically dictated for cold line applications, are

sometimes employed to keep the warm surface temperature above the dew point. See Section IX (Insulation Thickness Program) for condensation control calculations. The low temperature range is further divided into application classifications. A continuous vapor-retarder system is essential to the integrity of tie following classifications. 1. Cryogenic - Extreme Low •267.80C through •73.90C (4500F through .1010F) Water vapor which passes through the vapor-retarder will not only condense, but will freeze. Built up frost and ice will destroy the insulation system. 2. Refrigeration -73.30C through -0.60C (.1000F through 310F) Water vapor which passes through the vapor-retarder will not only condense, but will freeze. Built up frost and ice will destroy the insulation system. 3. Cold and Chilled Water OOC through 15.60C (320F through GOOF) Water vapor will condense on the metal causing corrosion and failure of the insulation system. The insulations generally used in these temperature ranges are: a. b. c. d. e. f. g. h. Cellular Glass. Elastomeric Glass fiber. Mineral Fiber. Phenolic (foamed). Polyolefin. Polyisocyanurate. Polyurethane. Polystyrene. See Insulation Materials Table I.A. INTERMEDIATE TEMPERATURE RANGE 16.10C to 315.60C (610F to 6000F) This temperature range includes conditions encountered in most industrial processes and the hot water and steam systems necessary in commercial installations. Selection of material in this range is based more on its thermal values than with low temperature applications. However, other factors such as mechanical and chemical properties, availability of forms,

installation time, and costs are also significant. The materials generally used in the intermediate range are: a. Calcium Silicate. b. Cellular Glass. c. Elastomeric. d. Expanded Silica, or Perlite. e. Glass Fiber. f. Mineral Fiber. g. Phenolic. h. Polystyrene. i. Polyurethane. j. Polyisocyanurate. k. Polyolefin. See Insulation Materials Table I.B. HIGH TEMPERATURE RANGE 31S.vc to 815.SOC (601* to 15000F) As the refractory range of insulation is approached, fewer materials and application methods are available. High temperæ ture materials are often a combination of other materials or similar materials manufactured using special binders. Jacketing is generally field applied. Industrial power and process piping and equipment, boilers, breechings, exhausts, and incinerators fall within this application range. The materials generally used are: a. Calcium Silicate. b. Cellular Glass. c. Cement. d. Ceramic Fiber. e. Glass Fiber. f. Mineral Fiber. g. Expanded Silica, or Perlite. See Insulation Materials Table I.C. 24

mra SECTION Ill INSULATION SYSTEMS DESIGN 3.1 GENERAL An insulation system is the combination of insulations, finishes and application methods which are used to achieve specific design objectives. Among these are: 1. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7. 8. 9. IO. 11. 12. Energy savings. Reduced operating costs. Condensation control. Chemical compatibility with the metals being insulated, the atmosphere to which the system will be exposed, and the various components of the insulation system itself. Protection of mechanical and insulation systems from mechanical abuse and atmospheric damage. Personnel protection. Fire safety. Sound control. Future requirements for access to piping, fittings, etc. Accommodation to limited clearances or work space. USDA hygienic requirements. Protect the environment by reducing emissions of particulates and greenhouse gasses. While there are several choices of insulation materials which meet basic thermal and cost-effective requirements of an installation, choices become more limited with each additional design objective that comes into play. In some cases the choice of outer coverings, accessories and installation methods is more affected by design objectives than the actual insulation material used. For instance, glass fiber pipe insulation is available with a variety of factory-applied jackets designed to resist different types of mechanically and chemically abusive atmospheres.

3.2 The use of pre-fabricated as opposed to field-fabricated insula- tion treatments of valves, flanges and fittings is a crucial factor in systems design. Design objectives which affect this decision include labor time, materials costs and provisions for future maintenance access to fittings. The choices are endless and require a working knowledge of insulation material properties as well as a firm grasp of the basic function of each insulation treatment, and the design conditions under which it must operate. Section III is presented as a guide to the general design objectives of both commercial and industrial installations. COMMERCIAL INSTALLATIONS Insulation in commercial buildings such as schools, shopping centers, warehouses, hospitals, hotels and other public buildings is designed primarily to reduce energy consumption and/or to prevent condensation.The types of mechanical systems commonly insulated in commercial installations vary only slightly from project to project, and involve a relatively narrow temperature range. Typical mechanical systems include: 1. Plumbing 12.80c to 82.20C (550Fto 1800F). 2. Ducts and housings 15.SOC to 43.30C (600Ftn 1 IOOF). a. Steam and condensate 98.90C to 185.00C (2100F to 3650F*) *upper limit will vary with the pressure of steam. 4. Outside air intake (seasonal outdoors temperature range). 5. Roof drains .60C to 15.SOC (330F to 600FJ. 6. Hot water heating 82.20C to 98.SOC to 2100F). 7. Chilled water 4.40C to 12.80C (400F to 550F). 8. Engine exhaust approximately 676.70C (12500F). 9. Kitchen exhaust ducts approximately 537.80C (10000F). 10. Refrigerant suction 4.40C to 10.OOC (400F to 500F). 11. Ammonia and liquid nitrogen -267.80C to •73.SOC (450 to -101 OF). 32

Care should be taken in designing commercial insulation sys- tems to specify the thickness, material and finish necessary to accomplish the purpose of the application. Commercial buildings are designed to accommodate either human comfort or materials storage.Generally these purposes are achieved through the proper design of the heating/ventilating/ cooling and plumbing systems. The purpose of the insulation system design is to enhance the efficiency of these systems, reduce energy consumption, help prevent damage from con- densation, sound control and fire containment. 3.2.1 DESIGN OBJECTIVES The insulation system designer must be aware of the objec- tives of the installation and the amount and type of equipment planned to achieve these objectives. In some cases, as with steam heating, proper insulation planning can reduce the required capacity of the generating system. (See Section IX — Economic Thickness Programs.) In the case of fruit and vegetable or refrigerated meat storage, temperature mainte- nance and condensation control objectives will supersede eco- nomic thickness design. Appearance and hygiene factors can also affect the choice of finishes in exposed areas and/or areas where food is being prepared or stored. (See Section 5.2.) Condensation control on ducts, chillers, roof drains and cold piping is a basic function of insulation in commercial buildings. Design objectives here are to choose materials and applica- tion methods which will achieve the best vapor-retardant seal possible, and to calculate the thickness of insulation necessary to prevent condensation. Insulation chosen for personnel protection and/or fire protection must be able to withstand high temperatures without contributing to a possible fire hazard. Engine exhausts which can reach temperatures of 454.40C to 676.7 OC (8500F to 12500F) should be insulated sufficiently to reduce surface temperatures exposed to personnel or flammable materials to under 60.00C (1400F). Kitchen exhaust ducts which are subjected to flammable grease accumulation fall within the same design criteria.

3.2.2 MATERIALS The insulation materials most widely used on commercial and industrial installations are: 1. 2. CALCIUM SILICATE (pipe insulation and block). — necessary for higher temperature range and instal- lations needing high density and rigidity such as high pressure steam and engine exhaust. GLASS a: Fibrous (pipe insulation, flexible blanket & board) available with various jacketing materials. — accommodates general commercial temperature range. b: Cellular (pipe insulation and block). — use on cold and hot surfaces. — use where a non-absorbent or vermin resistant material is requested. — use for underground applications. — accommodates the general commercial temper- ature range. MINERAL FIBER (pipe insulation and board). 3. — accommodates general commercial temperature range. EXPANDED SILICA, OR PERLITE (pipe insulation and 4. block). — accommodates intermediate and high temperature ranges. ELASTOMERIC (pipe insulation, sheets & rolls). 5. — no additional vapor-retarder or finish usually required — use on plumbing piping, HVAC systems, flexible lines and cold surfaces. FOAM PLASTIC (foamed-in-place, blocks and pipe 6. insulation). a: Polisocyanurate b: Polyolefins c: Polystyrenes — necessary where a more dense, less absorbent material is required. — accommodates the general commercial temperature range. 34

7. REFRACTORY FIBER — accommodates a higher temperature range and fire retaxiance. 8. SOLUBLE FIBER accommodates a higher temperawre range and fire retardance. g. INSULATING CEMENT — accommodates all above ambient temperature ranges. A variety of weather and vapor-retardant jackets and mastics is available to aid insulation materials in meeting design objectives such as fire safety, appearance and system abuse protection. 3.2.3 APPLICATION METHODS Section IV illustrates the general application methods appro- priate for installation of insulation materials, finishes and securements on commercial projects. NOTE: The ambient temperature and humidity conditions of the installation are factors which may alter the number and types of securement specified. Mechanical systems should be properly sealed and tested before insulation is applied. 3.2.4 SPECIFICATIONS Because commercial installations involve relatively limited materials and applications choices, with few variations to the mechanical systems receiving insulation treatment, there is a tendency to prepare general specifications which are often insufficient for proper installation or bidding. For example, a specification which states that "chillers or all cold surfaces of chillers will be insulated " can be interpreted in several ways especially if it hasn't been designated as to whether or not the chiller has been factory insulated. All materials, thicknesses, finishes, securements and design objectives should be carefully communicated to the insulation contractor. (See Section VI — Specification Writing.)

3.4 INDUSTRIAL INSTALLATIONS Conditions exist in industrial installations such as power plants, chemical plants, petroleum refineries, steel, pulp and paper mills, meat packing plants, food, soap, and cosmetic process plants, marine work, etc., which require that the insulation systems designer be involved in the project during the design phase. Depending upon the industrial process or function of the installation, these conditions include: 1. Stringent control of extreme temperature parameters. 2. Corrosive atmospheres resulting from the presence of process chemicals or the location of equipment and piping outdoors. Increased fire hazard caused by high temperatures and 3. the presence of volatile substances. Presence of operating personnel. (personnel protection) 4. Sanitary and contamination requirements for food, meat 5. packing, soap, cosmetic, dairy and brewery processes. Additional mechanical abuse to insulations from excessive 6. handling, foot traffic on vessel tops and lines, and the added movement of expansion, contraction and vibration. Necessity for easy removal of insulation for predictable 7. maintenance areas. Critical clearance and space limitations coupled with 8. the need for greater thickness of insulations. Complex construction and installation schedules. 9. Radiation hazards in nuclear facilities. IO. Work accessibility requiring scaffolding, cranes, etc. 11. Pertinent data concerning the installation design objectives, the materials being processed or used, applicable government regulations or codes, operating data and temperature para- meters must be determined far enough in advance of final specification preparation to insure the design of a properly functioning insulation systems

3.5 DATA REQUIRED FOR INDUSTRIAL INSULATION SYSTEMS DESIGN 3.5.1 NATURE OF THE PROCESS The possibility of spillage, leaks and accidental contamination of process chemicals and products is always present in industrial installations. Insulations should be chosen which do not react to the chemicals contained in the vessels or piping to which they are applied. Such a reaction may lower the ignition temperature of the process chemical or insulation material, contributing to fire hazard conditions. Special care should be taken to use non-absorbent insulation in the presence of combustible or toxic liquids. Spontaneous combustion of a combustible liquid absorbed over the large surface area of insulation may occur as it oxidizes. Absorbent insulation may contribute significantly to an accidental fire by storing up the spilled or leaked combustible materials. USDA Standards for food, soap and cosmetic manufacturing plants prohibit use of insulations which sliver or dust, are toxic, or contain glass. Equipment, piping and insulation must be provided with finishes which will not support fungus, mildew or bacteria growth. The finish must resist washing down with high pressure water, steam and detergents without appreciable deterioration. In meeting IJSDA requirements, plastic laminates and finishes excel in their resistance to fungal and bacterial growth although their temperature and mechanical strength limitations pose problems in high pressure wash down areas and under long periods of steam cleaning. Stainless steel is the most appropriate of the metal jacketing materials, having high resistance to corrosives and bacterial growth as well as high mechanical strength. Aluminum may erode in wash down areas or where strong cleaning chemicats are used. The use of weather and vapor retardance coatings, reinforced with glass cloth or mesh, provides a mechanically strong and sanitary finish for equipment and other irregular surfaces. Many are also resistant to chemicals. 39

3.5.2 SPECIFIC TEMPERATURE PARAMETERS OF PIPING AND EQUIPMENT In addition to the reduction of heat loss or gain, industrial insulation systems must maintain controlled temperatures required for process materials being transported from one point in a facility to another. Temperature conwol may be continuous, intermitent, cyclic or rapidly changed due to weather conditions or the necessib/ of steam cleaning and wash down periods. An insulation of high thermal diffusivity, low specific heat and low density is desirable in installations which require rapid heat-up or cool-off of insulated surfaces. A process changing from hot to cold every few minutes requires an insulation that has the ability to change temperature quickly and has very low mass to retain heat. The temperature of an insulation's outer surface must be considered where insulation is used for personnel protection or where excessive surface temperatures might cause igni- tion of fumes or gases. On low temperature installations, surface temperatures must be above dew point to prevent condensation and drip. The emissivity property of insulation finishes is significant in these cases. High emissivity is recom- mended on finishes used for personnel protection treatrnents. On installations where temperatures must be maintained at specific levels, it must be decided in the design phase whether added insulation thickness or heat tracing would provide the most efficient service. This decision is based on data other than the conventional Economic Thickness considerations. Extreme temperature surfaces in industrial process and power facilities may require the use of materials and applica- tion methods which can absorb expansion, contraction and vibration movement. Stainless steel banding or expansion bands are recommended for applications with extreme expansion movement or on large diameter surfaces. Because most high temperature insulations shrink while the metal surface expands, methods such as double layer — staggered joint construction, the design and placement of cushioned expansion joints and/or the use of high rib lath between insulation and metal surfaces may be employed to protect the insulation seal.

Awareness of the nature of the process, its components, the relative temperatures of piping and equipment and the general location of such equipment and substances, aids the specifier in determining areas where excess heat or chemicals may create fire hazards or personnel hazards. 3.5.3 METAL SURFACES RECEIVING INSULATION TREATMENT A selected insulation should not be chemically reactive to the metal over which it is applied. Basically, insulation installed on steel should be neutral or slightly alkaline. That installed on aluminum should be neutral or slightly acidic. External stress corrosion cracking of stainless steel may result from the presence of chloride ions on its surface. Insulation containing chlorides or located in a salt-laden or chloride contaminated atmosphere must not be in direct contact with unprotected stainless steel jacketing or surfaces. In the case of stainless steel jacketing, factory-applied moisture barriers on the inner surface may be sufficient protection. Insulation systems must be designed to prevent possible galvanic cell corrosion to metal piping and equipment. Some industrial. high temperature insulation materials contain salts which, when moist, setup a low voltage galvanic cell with the iron pipe or vessel wall as the positive pole and metal jacketing as the negative pole. This action results in either the pipe/ vessel wall or the jacket, sacrificing itself to the point of Humidity levels, temperatures and salt content must, be considered when specifying insulation materials, masr.•t jacketing, and accessories. 3.5.4 OPERATING DATA The location of instruments and maintenance areas where personnel will be present is significant when specifying treatments for personnel protection and materials abuse protection from foot traffic, excessive handling and opera- tional machinery. Rigid insulation materials and jacketing are recommended in these areas. High pressure wash down areas require resistance to water and detergents as well as high mechanical strength. 1

3.5.5 FUTURE ACCESS AND MAINTENANCE REQUIREMENTS Leaks are most likely to occur at valves, füngs and flanges. Low temperature insulation can be protected from leaks by sealing off adjacent insulation with vapor-retardance mastics. Removable flting covers may be specified at predictable maintenance areas, while special leak detection mechanisms may be installed at other locations. However, on hot applicæ tions a rigid inspection and replacement program is the best prevention of large scale insulation destruction due to leakage. Turbines, pumps, valves, etc., which require easy access for inspection and maintenance, can be insulated with removable insulation blankets fabricated from stainless steel mesh or high temperature fabric filled With fibrous insulation. These are attached to the surfaces by means of metal eyelets built into the blankets around the edges. 3.5.s The floor level of large tanks can be protected from spilled chemical or water from wash downs by using a nonabsorbent insulation along the bottom skirt or support, or by sealing with caulking. ATMOSPHERIC CONDITIONS The atmosphere surrounding industrial piping and equipment presents additional problems in the selection of finishes and jacketing. Of particular concern is the presence of chemicals or humidity which act to corrode metal finishes. Because of its excellent weather-barrier and mechanical properties, metal jacketing is widely used on industrial installations. The metals most resistant to corrosive atmos- pheric conditions and humidity are stainless and coated steels. Coated aluminum can be used to combat specific conditions by selection of the exact coating required. However, the coatings are not always abrasive resistant, leaving the aluminum open to attack at fastener openings, cuts, etc. Aluminum is weather resistant but does not always hold up in wash down areas or where strong cleaning chemicals are used. Factory spray-applied moisture-barriers are recommended on aluminum jacketing. The coverings considered most resistant to corrosives and abrasive chemicals are the plastic types. Unless protected.

3.5.7 some PVC type coverings may break down when subjected to the effects of ozone, infra-red and ultra-violet rays. Protective paints are available for PVC coverings not manufactured for outdoor use. Weather barrier coatings offer good protection from weather as well as from the chemical attack of acids, alkalies, solvents and salts, either air-borne or as a result of intermitent spillage. Glass cloth and other fabric membranes, used in conjunction with the appropriate mastics, are generally used as reinforcements and add mechanical strength to the installation. Maximum protection from chemical atack on cold and dual temperature service is achieved through the use of vapor- retardant coatings. They, too, are applied with reinforcing fabric. Stainless steel jackets and bands are recommended in areas which require superior fire resistance. Stainless steel is rec- ommended over the use of aluminum due to the latter's lower melting point. Factory spray-applied moisture barriers are recommended on stainless steel jacketing. Some weather and vapor-retardant mastics also offer fire retardant proper- ties to an insulation system. Flexible membranes are field or factory applied to the insula- tion and provides mechanical, chemical, thermal or moisture protection of varying levels. CLEARANCES Because of the complexity of process piping and the added thickness required to control heat loss or gain, clearances often become so minimal that it may be necessary to insulate piping together in groups. This is also true in marine work. 3.5.8 SCHEDULING AND MATERIALS STORAGE Precise industrial installation schedules and good application practice often dictate that insulation be finished as soon as possible after roughing-in. The materials chosen must have the necessary strength to resist an excessive amount of handling and moving at the installation site. Materials which are moisture absorbent must also be protected from water while being stored at the site. Storage areas should be clearly indicated for the insulation contractor in project specifications, and should be noted as covered or open.

3.5.9 3.5.10 SPECIFICATIONS Contract drawings should indicate the extent and general arrangement of the site and the process piping to receive insulation treatment. The size of piping and equipment, Jine origination and termination, elevations, support locations, and orientation of nozzles, fittings and valves should also be indicated and properly dimensioned. QUALITY OF MATERIALS Insulation and associated materials should be specified and ordered to meet appropriate codes and standards. Manufacturers' data sheets and test reports should be consulted in the selection process to determine conformity. The building envelope should be closed and sealed prior to the installation of the insulation materials. 4

muca SECTION IV GENERAL APPLICATION METHODS/ILLUSTRATIONS 4.1 INTRODUCTION TO THE PLATES The illustrations in this section represent applications deemed acceptable by members of the insulation industry. They illustrate the application of a certain form (i.e. pipe insulation, blanket) rather than type (i.e. cellular glass, mineral fiber) of insulation, on particular installations. Several alternative application methods and material choices are represented for each type of installation. For example, elbows may be insulated using either a pre-formed elbow. mitered segments of insulation, or a PVC/glass fiber elbow system. The combinations of insulation materials, finishes and secure- ments illustrated in the plates should not be interpreted as prescriptive or recommended installations. Although the insulation systems illustrated represent correct practices and compatible materials choices, they do not represent the only acceptable combinations. In an attempt to cover as many of the design conditions possible in any one installation, many illustrations depict a more complete application than is neces- sary. For instance, concealed installations often do not require finishes or securements as shown on some of the plates. These choices are dependent on several design criteria more clearly discussed in Sections II and III. The purpose of Section IV is to visualize for the specifier, installer and inspector the correct application of materials on specific installations. A specifying engineer may use the illustra- tions to communicate to the contractor, installer or inspector how the completed installation should appear, eliminating potential misunderstandings or confusion. He should correspond his list of materials, thicknesses and application notes to the numbers on the illustration. The contractor may use the drawings in a similar manner when preparing submittals, proposals or when communicating instructions to installation personnel.

4.2 PIPING PLATES NO. 1 THROUGH 9 Piping systems covered in this secuon represent the majorty of the commercial & industnal insulation conü•adors work. These systems normally range in temperature between OOC and 537.BOC (320F and IOOOOFJ. The followng is a basc classification of piping systems usually found in this temperature range: CLASSIFICATIONS & MATERIALS USED COLD PIPING OOC through 15.60C t320F through GOOF) Systems within this range include: Chemical Refrigerant Glycol Dual temperature Make up water Chilled water Drain Process Cold water MODERATE PIPING IS.IOC through ss.40C (610F through 211'fl Systems within this range include: Domestic hot water Heating hot water Condensate return Process Dual temperature Traced Piping HOT PIPING 100.OOC through 537.20C (2120F through SSSOF) Systems within this range include: Steam High pressure condensate High temperature hot water Process & utility MATERIALS All the basic pipe insulation materials found on the Insulation Material Tables I-A & I-B are suitable for the applications found in Plates No. 1 through 9. GENERAL APPLICATION Application of pre-formed pipe insulation may be in single or multiple layers. In the majority of installations, single layer construction is used. However, multiple layer construction may be required on large sized pipes, long runs of piping or piping at extreme low or high temperatures. Joints should be staggered and the layers properly secured. See Plate No. 1. Pipe insulation is available in many forms. Jacketing is either factory-applied or field applied. Variations in application method occur mainly due to the type of finish and support/hanger used, rather than the generic make-up of the pipe insulation itself. COLD PIPING Vapor-retarder design is a major consideration on low temperature and cold piping. In order to isolate vapor- retarder failures on low temperature piping, it is sometimes necessary to apply a vapor seal to butt joints at specified points along the line. Multiple layer construction is often necessary on low temperature and refrigerated piping. For extreme low temperature applica- tions see Section V, Plates No. 41 and 42. MODERATE AND HOT PIPING Single layer construction is sufficient for moderate or hot piping. Double layer construction or expansion joints may be specified on extremely hot piping. See Plate No. 9. SUPPORTS AND PIPE HANGERS At supports, the pipe insulation section should extend past the shield or support mechanism to facilitate proper sealing of butt joints. See Plate No.6. Hanger rods or protrusions on cold piping must be insulated to prevent condensation from forming on the rod. Condensation insulation should extend up the rod approximately four times the thickness of the pipe insulation. In general, however, ring hangers or hanger/ supports attached directly to the pipe are not recommended on cold piping. The recommended method is illustrated on Plate No. 6. 7
SECUREMENTS Use of mechanical fasteners, adhesives, taper and bands, etc., is dependent upon the jacketing material used and the temperature/ humidity conditions of the installation. See Section II.

PLATE NO. 1 PIPING Pre-formed Pipe Insulation Multiple Layer Construction Multiple layer construction denotes the choice of using more than one layer of insulation rather than choosing a single layer of equivalent thickness. The decision to use multiple layers may be made for one or more of the following reasons: (A) To provide compensaüon for pipe expansion and contraction. (B) To reduce heat flow by staggering joints, thus creating a more thermally efficient installation. (C) To achieve thickness in excess of manufacturers' capabilities. (D) For retrofitting purposes. To obtain optimum insulation value at joints, each layer is stag- gered when applied. Each layer must be secured in place before the next is applied. PLATE NO. 8 PIPING Traced Piping Tracers provide heat for piping which must be protected from freezing, and for process piping which must be maintained at a minimum temperature. The two most common types are (1) tubing which runs horizontally along the length of the piping, and (2) electrical cables or tape coiled around the pipe. Tracers may be thermally connected to the pipe by means of heat transfer cement, or simply banded to the pipe. The exact type and length of tracing should be indicated in the specification as it may affect the size of pipe insulation neces- sary and/or the application time which must be estimated. The insulation material chosen should insulate efficiently at the temperature of the tracer as well as that of the pipe. Jacketing materials must be weather-proof if tracing is located outdoors. Plate No. 8 illustrates the use of oversized pipe insulation on traced piping. Other applications include the use of wrap insulations and specially made extended leg pipe insulations. Detail A - Tubing tracer without heat transfer cement. Detail B - Tubing tracer with heat transfer cement. Detail C - Double tubing tracer without heat transfer cement. FITTINGS, VALVES, FLANGES AND MECHANICAL COUPLINGS 4.3 PLATES No. 10 THROUGH 18 Insulation of fitings, valves, flanges and couplings is the most time consuming and often most expensive aspect of commercial and industrial insulation. Choices of materials and application methods must be based on various design criteria discussed in Sections II and III. CLASSIFICATIONS FITTINGS — Items used to change size, direction of flow, level or assembly of piping, except for unions, flanges, valves or strainers. They may be of the screwed, sweat or welded types. See Plate No. 10, 11, 12, & 13. VALVES — Any of various devices that regulate liquid or gas flow. They may be of the screwed, sweated, flanged, or welded types. see Plate No. 14, 15 & 16. FLANGES — Protruding rims and edges of the screwed or sweated type used with fittings, valves, couplings, etc. See Plate No. 17. MECHANICAL COUPLINGS — Devices used in assembly of piping. see Plate No. 18. Screwed and flanged connections on fittings, valves, couplings, etc. usually require oversized insulation applications to compensate for the protrusions. see Plate No. 12, 14, 15, 17, & 18. GENERAL APPLICATIONS Depending upon design conditions and costs, the following application methods may be used on both hot and cold surfaces. 1 .Pre-formed rigid fiting insulation. Produced in two halves and secured directly onto the fiting, valve, etc. See Plate No. IO &

2. 3. 4. 5. 6. Mitered or fabricated füing insulation. Can be pre-fabricated or field-fabricated out of pieces of pre-formed pipe insulation, sheet or block and installed directly onto the fiting, valve, etc. See Plate No. 11, 14 & 17. Glass fiber/PVC insulation and cover systems. One piece PVC fitting, valve and coupling covers are secured over glass fiber flexible blanket insulation. See Plate No. 12, 15 & 18. Cement applied to irregular surfaces. Applicabon may or may not require reinforcement mesh or fabric depending on fitng size, insulation thickness and other considerations. Mineral fiber cement is used for high temperature applications. One-coat finishing cement is used on moderate temperatures or as a finish to other insulations. See Plate No. IO, 13 & 16. Glass fiber blanket insulation wrapped around füngs, valves and couplings and secured. Cold applications must be vapor sealed. (Not illustrated). Foamed-in-place urethane used on irregular surfaces as a fill insulation inside fitting, valve and coupling covers. use as per manufacturer's directions. (Not illustrated). MATERIALS The same basic insulation and finishing materials used on piping can be used on hot and cold fittings, valves and couplings. Choice of materials must be based on various design criteria discussed in Sections II & III. COVERS AND FINISHES The finishes shown are not intended to be prescriptive. Selections should be based on temperature, exposure and appearance requirements. Finishes may be prefabricated as with metal and PVC systems shown on Plate No. 10, 11, 12, 14, 15, 17 & 18 or field-applied combination of mastics, vapor-retarders, cements, fabrics and mesh as shown on Plate No. 10, 13, 16 & 17.

DUCTS, PLENUMS & HOUSINGS 4.4 PLATES NO. 19 THROUGH 24 The terms "ducts, plenums and housings" refer to those rectangular, round and oval sheet metal structures which function as part of commercial heating. ventilating & cooling systems by enclosing air moving equipment and low, medium and high velocity air. Insulation contractors are concerned primarily with those ducts, plenums and housings which require field application of insulation materials. The functions of duct, plenum and housing insulations are to: 1. Retard heat loss or gain to the air being carried through the systems. 2. Prevent condensation on cold air ducts, etc. a. Prevent high temperature surfaces from becoming fire or per sonnel safety hazards. 4. Provide acoustical and/or sound attenuation for the syst(2'f The first three functions require application of insulation mc.t.- the exterior surfaces. Acoustical lining of ducts with special irr, absorbing material reduces airborne noise inside the Acoustical lining of the duct, however, is not always vent condensation or provide the necessary thermal Absorbing and sound barrier material installed inside or ; system reduces noise transmission to surrounding areas. 7

1 CLASSIFICATIONS The following classifications of ducts, plenums and housings help in determining the general temperature ranges that may be encountered: Cooling supply and return Heating supply and return Outside air Mixed air Exhaust air (specify hot or cold) MATERIALS Choice of materials must be based on various design criteria discussed in Sections II & III. FINISHES AND JACKETING The choice of materials depends upon the temperature of the outside surface of the insulation as well as the temperature and humidity of the surrounding air and whether indoors or outdoors. fire hazards and mechanical abuse must also be considered. Vapor retarder finishes are often factory applied, but these applications require joints and anchor locations to be properly sealed. GENERAL APPLICATIONS Glass and mineral fiber blankets and boards are secured with adhesives or mechanical fasteners. Elastomeric foam insulation is installed with special contact adhesives. Calcium silicate and expanded vermiculite are attached with mechanical fasteners, tie wires or bands. Cellular glass block is installed with bedding mastics, mechanical fasten- ers or bands. Field installations and acoustical lining require special securement devices relative to the velocity of the air in the system. 88

VESSELS, TANKS AND EQUIPMENT 4.5 PLATES NO. 25 THROUGH 35 The term equipment applies to rectangular, cylindrical or irregular shaped mechanical devices which function within the heating/ ventilating/cooling. plumbing, steam, or process systems of industrial and commercial facilities. CLASSIFICATIONS Cold OOC to 21.10C (320F to 700F) Water coolers Water chillers Chilled water pumps Domestic and process cold water equipment Chilled water storage tanks Hot 21.70C to 315.60C (71 OF to 6000F) Hot water storage tanks Hot water pumps Fuel oil heaters Deaerators/condensate receivers Booster heaters Heat exchangers Hot water converters or generators Power and process equipment Extreme Above 315.SOC (6000F) and below OOC (320F)) Boilers Power and process equipment (turbines, stacks, etc.) Refrigerant equipment (accumulators, suction traps, etc.) Refrigerant tanks (liquid nitrogen, ammonia, brine, etc.) Emergency generator exhaust 1 og

MATERIALS USED Choice of materials must be based on various design criteria discussed in Sections II & III. GENERAL APPLICATION 1. Blocks and mitered pipe insulation segments (see Plate No. 25, 29, 29A, 30 S 31). 2. Rigid board (See Plate No. 26). 3. Rigid wrap-around glass fiber (See Plate No. 27). 4. Elastomeric sheets (See Plate No. 28). 5. Flexible blanket (See Plate No. 25). 6. Pre-engineered panel systems — apply according to manufactur- ers' instructions. 7. Cement — reinforced insulating cements finished with fabric, may be used on pumps or other irregular equipment surfaces. Cement covers the body of the equipment, tapering off and finished at fitting areas for easy access. Mineral fiber cement is used for high temperature applications. One-coat cement may be used in moderate temperatures or as a finish to other insulations. Cold applications require an additional vapor-retarder treatment. SECUREMENT Mechanical fasteners may or may not be used on vessel surfaces depending on the design of the vessel, its surface temperature and the fire hazard potential involved. Stainless steel bands are recom- mended for securing blocks, board or metal jacketing on applications which (1) cannot use surface mechanical fasteners, (2) involve large diameters or (3) are subject to significant expansion and contraction. Stainless steel is recommended on these installations because of its strength and resistance to corrosion. Insulation supports are recommended on large vessels and tanks and should be installed by the vessel fabricator. Special consideration should be given prior to welding on vessels, tanks & equipment. The use of expansion springs where vessel is subject to significant expansion and contraction is recommended. FITTINGS See "Fittings. etc." for proper application methods on equipment fittings. Nozzle and manhole cover insulation is illustrated on Plate No. 33. Ill

5.3 N0EE AND SOUND CONTROL INSULATION SYSTEMS The phenomenon of sound and is divided into categories 1. Acoustical Treatment 2. Sound Attenuation Acoustical treatment deals with limiting the reflection or echo of sound by application of certain absorbent materials to ceilings, walls and floors. Acoustical treatment allows the originating sound to be heard more clearly and distinctly. The materials used and the methods of installation are not in the scope of this text. Sound attenuation is the limiting of sound propagation from one area to another. This requires the application of special and standard insulation materials to encase or enclose une noise generating source, forming a sound barrier beüeen the source and the surrounding area. Since this is the work of the insulation contractor, this section will describe the appropriate materials and methods used. The purposes of applying sound atenuation materials are to: 1. Reduce obnoxious noise levels emiting from machines, equip- ment, pipe lines or enclosures. 2. Reduce general noise level in plant areas. 3. Provide for better work conditions. 4. Comply with noise level standards of OSHA and EPA. 5.3.1 UNDERSTANDING SOUND Sound is composed of two physical properties; Loudness — Intensity measured in decibels (dB) Tone — Frequency measured in Hertz (Hz) Since sound is energy, it can be expressed in various forms, i.e., ergs/sec., wats, ft.-lbs./sec. We can measure the pressure varia- tion and the frequency of the occurrence that produce sound. When sound is conducted through a media, the variations of the impulses cause a compression and rarefaction of the molecules in the substance. Air is our most common media and the compression and rarefaction of the gas by the impression of sound causes a variation

in pressure. At atmospheric pressure (14.7 PS!) the variation of one millionth of an atmosphere (Micro Bar) produces a rather loud noise (74 dB). By physically testing the human ears' response to pressure variations, it is determined that a pressure change of .0002 Micro-bars can no longer be distinguished and no sound is heard. This is equivalent 1012 watts of electrical power. These numbers become the reference values for measuring sound. The measurement of intensity and frequency and its relationship to sound pressure, sound power and acoustical energy must be understood. 5.3.2 THE DECIBEL The "Bel" is a dimensionless ratio of electrical energy in logarithmic terms of 10. This over compresses the scale for practical use so the Decibel (dB) is used: 1 Bei = 10 dB For Electrical Reference the Power Watt Level (PWL) is used: PWL = 10 Log. dB R•fe.n— For Pressure Reference and since Intensity varies as the square of pressure, the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is used: SPL = 20 Log. dB To realize the energy ratio to the Decibel, assume; 10 units 100 units 1,000 units 10.000 units 100.000 units 60 dB 70 dB 80 dB 90 dB - 100 dB The total energy input at 100 dB is .01 watts. To reduce the noise level to 90 dB 90,000 units or 90% must be dissipated. To reduce to 80 dB 99.000 units or 99% of the energy has to be attenuated.

5.3.3 FREaUENCY Sound is also a variety of tones and the ear is sensitive to these different pitches. Normal ear sensitMty occurs in the range of 80 to 14,000 cps or Hertz (hz). Musical custom measures tones in notes, B notes to an octave, each respective note being twice the frequency of its preceding octave. The sound or noise we are concerned with is a combination of all discordant frequencies and is often referred to as "white noise.n The center frequency of each octave identifies the bands as follows; 63 Hz. 1st Octave Band 125 Hz. 2nd Octave Band 250 Hz. 3rd Octave Band 500 Hz. 4th Octave Band 1000 Hz. 5th Octave Band 2000 Hz. 6th Octave Band 4000 Hz. 7th Octave Band 8000 Hz. 8th Octave Band The combinations of intensity and frequency produces a damping of the hearing sensitivity in the lower octave band ranges. The energy required to produce sound at 70 dB @ 62.5 Hz is the same as 70 dB @ 2000 Hz but the ear does not respond quite that accurately. Again, by physical testing, the lower frequencies are automatically attenuated in the ear so that the energy or pressure required to produce 90 dB at 2000 Hz is heard at 80 dB at 62.5 Hz. For this reason various scales are incorporated in the dB meter. 5.3.4 THE SOUND METER The sound meter must hear all noise frequencies and intens levels and calculate the summation as a dB reading. Since circuitry is electronic the pressure waves are converted ele&ical impulses. These can be conb•olled to exact linear proporti0! and when so done the scale is called "C". When partial damping filte• for the low frequency range are installed the scale is "B". To mos. nearly match the human ear, full weighted filters for the low frequencies calibrate the "A" scale. The readings then taken are listed as dBA. A further enhancement of the meter is the octave band analyzer. Circuitry that filters all frequencies except the one being tested gives the exact dB level for that band. By testing all octave bands and recording the level for each a profile of the white noise is obtained and this is important in selecting the proper treatment. 51

5.3.5 DESCRIPTION OF NOISE The human ear normally responds to tones which can be classified into three basic ranges. RANGE 1. Low Frequency EXAMPLES LEVEL 50 - 500 Hz. Hum, Rumble, Growl 500- 4000 Hz. Howl, Clang, Horn 2. Mid Frequency 3. High Frequency 4000 - 14000 Hz. The human ear is also sensitive to sound intensity. Various common sounds and decibel levels are: LEVEL RANGE 1 . Threshold of sound O - 40 dB 2. Moderate to loud 50 - 70 dB 3. Loud and objectionable 80 - 90 dB 4. Harmful - possible 100 - 1 IO dB ear damage 120 dB 5. Threshold of pain EXAMPLES Faint whisper Average office/ normal conversation Street noise/factory Boiler factory/air hammer/ large engines Explosion/cannonading/ jet aircraft OSHA has established certain periods of time during which an individual may be exposed to different noise levels in excess of 90 dBA. Noise level dBA indicates the sound level is measured on the "A" scale of the meter. The following table gives noise level exposure allowed. NOISE LEVEL dBA ALLOWABLE DAILY EXPOSURE 90 92 95 97 100 102 105 110 115 8 Hours 6 4 2 1.5 .5 15 Seconds 5.3.B PROPERTIES OF MATERIALS THAT ATTENUATE SOUND Sound is acoustical energy and even though small amounts produce very loud noises, (BO dB represents .0001 watts) attenuating or dissipating it requires conversion to another form - heat,

Sound Barriers There are many ways to construct a sound barrier with the use of mate- rials that do not conduct or transmit sound. The ideal construction for a sound barrier is a limp mass wall or enclosure around the noise source. Basic materials are: 1. Barium loaded vinyls, neoprene, or other dense laminated material. 2. Heavy elastomeric coatings and caulking. 3. Cement plaster, plaster board or poured concrete. The effect of a sound barrier on a noise source. Reflected Noise Barrier Noise Source Transmitted Noise SOUND BARRIER The effectiveness of the barrier will generally improve with increasing thickness of the insulation layer and increasing the unit mass of sound barrier layer. For example, increasing the insulation layer frort to 4" and/or increasing the mass of the barrier material from to 2 lb/ft2 or greater will provide improved acoustic isolation. The difference between the intensity of the noise source and the mitted noise is called Sound Transmission Loss (STL). All sound materials are tested for their characteristic STL. Table #1 demonso:• Sound Transmission Loss for a certain type of material. 153

TABLE #1 Frequency (Octzve Bands) Hz 125 250 500 1000 2000 4000 STC Sound Transmission Loss 15 19 21 28 33 37 27 Note that STL varies with different frequencies. This parücu!ar material is more effective at the higher range than in the mid or Iow range. Since different materials will have different STL values. it is convenient to have a single number or classification rating. This is a single number simpli- fication derived from the STL date that is heavily weighted in the 500 to 2000 Hz frequency bands called Sound Transmission Class or STC. STC is a number that is used to rate doors, windows, wall types, and acoustic partitions with regard to their effecüveness in providing privacy against instrusion of noise in speech frequency only. The higher une STC the better the sound barrier. Although generally this may not be the case when dealing with low frequency noise problems such as caused by fans and compressors. Although this term is in common use in the field, take care when applying materials rated in tern-ts of STC when addressing mechanical or industrial noise problems. Sound Absorbers Materials that are soft and of open granular or cellular construction, readily receive sound waves, but reflect very litle. If the material is Ü'lick enough, some sound is dissipated throughout its structure but most is transmitted. The remaining sound is reflected. Basic materials are: 1. Glass fiber blanket or board. 2. Mineral wool blanket or board. 3. Polyurethane foam (open cell). 4. Polyester foam (open cell). 5. Felted materials. The effect of a sound absorber on a noise source. Reflected Noise Noise Source Absorber Material Transmited Noise ABSORBER 154

Most of the noise is transmitted, some is dissipated in the material and some is reflected. The ratio of acoustical energy absorbed and transmitted to that of the noise source is called Sound Absorption Coefficient (SAC). Materials are tested for this characteristic and results published. See Table #2 for a typical absorbent material. TABLE #2 Frequency (Octave Bands) Hz 125 250 500 1000 2000 4000 NRC Sound Absorption Coefficient ag 59 87 84 73 59 75 Note that SAC varies with the frequency. Again, it is convenient to have one single number represent the product. The arithmetic average of the SAC, measured at 250, 500, 1000, and 2000 Hz. is published as the Noise Reduction Coefficient (NRC) for that product. This is a dimensionless number and its value is in comparing the effectiveness of various absorbers. The larger the NRC, the better the absorption. Composite and Decoupled Barriers In most instances one single material or method is not sufficient to atenuate or reduce the noise to an acceptable level. The combination of an absorber and a barrier substantially increases the effectiveness of the treatment. The larger the NRC, the better the absorption capability. Damping Walls, surfaces and areas can be excited to vibrate and become secondary noise sources by sound or mechanical conduction. All objects have an inherent resonant or critical frequency that can be energized by a similar noise frequency. This sympathetic vibration can be eliminated by changing the resonant frequency above the audible range. This can be accomplished by: I. Stiffening or bracing. 2. Isolating conductors. 3. Application of dampening materials to the surface. Damping materials are generally the same as sound barriers although some manufacturers make special damping coatings. The material should be limp and relatively massive. The thickness of coatings should be in accordance with manufacturers' recommendations. Surface preparation is extremely important as adhesion of the coating is an essential part of damping. II
